title: Functional Medicine and the Rage Workup
dek: When you sit in the practitioner's office and they hand you a list of supplements for your adrenal fatigue, they are looking at the smoke, not the fire. Your body is not broken; it is responding exactly as it should to a world that demands your silence. Here is how to read the labs through the lens of your own fury.
bodyMarkdown:
## The Waiting Room

You are sitting in a chair that is slightly too soft, in a room that smells faintly of lavender and clinical-grade disinfectant. The functional medicine practitioner is looking at your lab results. They are talking about cortisol curves, about DHEA, about thyroid antibodies and gut permeability. They are telling you that your HPA axis is dysregulated. They are telling you that you are in a state of chronic sympathetic dominance. 

You nod. You take notes. You look at the printout with its red and yellow bars indicating where your body has failed to maintain homeostasis. You feel a familiar tightening in your jaw, right at the masseter muscle. It is the same tightening you feel when your boss interrupts you in a meeting, the same tightening you feel when your partner leaves the dishes in the sink again, the same tightening you feel when you read the news. 

They are giving you a protocol. Ashwagandha for the adrenals. L-glutamine for the gut. Magnesium for the tension. You will take these pills. You will do the deep breathing exercises. You will try to sleep eight hours a night. But as you walk out to your car, your keys clutched tight in your fist, your trapezius muscles pulled up toward your ears, you know that the protocol is missing something fundamental. 

They are treating the smoke. They are not looking at the fire. 

Your body is not broken. Your adrenal glands are not fatigued because they are weak. They are exhausted because they have been running a marathon for twenty years, pumping out stress hormones to keep you safe in a world that constantly demands your compliance and punishes your boundaries. Your gut is not permeable because you ate the wrong kind of bread. It is permeable because your nervous system has been locked in a state of hypervigilance, diverting blood flow away from digestion and toward your limbs so you can fight or flee. 

But you never fight. And you never flee. You just sit there, and you smile, and you swallow the rage. 

## The Biology of Unexpressed Fury

Let us be very clear about what anger is. It is not a moral failing. It is not a spiritual defect. It is not a low-vibration emotion that you need to meditate away. Anger is biology. It is a massive mobilization of physiological resources designed to protect your boundaries and ensure your survival. 

When a boundary is crossed, your amygdala sounds the alarm. Your hypothalamus signals your pituitary gland, which signals your adrenal glands. Adrenaline and noradrenaline flood your bloodstream. Your heart rate increases. Your blood pressure rises. Your breathing becomes shallow and rapid. Glucose is released into your blood for quick energy. Your muscles tense, preparing for action. 

This is a brilliant, elegant system. It is designed to last for a few minutes. You fight off the predator, or you run away, and then your nervous system discharges the energy and returns to a state of rest and digest. 

But what happens when the predator is your mortgage? What happens when the predator is systemic misogyny? What happens when the predator is a culture that tells you that your worth is determined by your productivity and your compliance? 

You cannot punch your mortgage. You cannot outrun systemic misogyny. So the mobilization happens, the energy rises, the muscles tense—and then... nothing. The energy has nowhere to go. It gets trapped in the body. 

Gabor Maté, a physician who has spent decades studying the link between stress and disease, writes extensively about this phenomenon. He notes that when we repress our emotions, particularly anger, we are not just pushing down a feeling; we are suppressing our immune system. In his work, Maté explains that the chronic repression of healthy anger leads to a state where the body's defenses turn against themselves. The immune system, confused by the constant state of alarm and the lack of a clear external enemy, begins to attack the body's own tissues. This is the root of many autoimmune conditions, which disproportionately affect women. 

Maté's observations are not just theoretical; they are grounded in the hard reality of physiology. When you swallow your anger, you are swallowing a biological imperative. You are forcing your body to contain an explosion. And over time, that containment takes a toll. The muscles stay tense. The fascia tightens. The cortisol keeps pumping, eventually leading to the very dysregulation your functional medicine doctor is trying to treat with supplements. 

## The Somatic Reality of the Labs

Let us look at those lab results again, but this time, let us read them through the lens of your unexpressed fury. 

**The Cortisol Curve:** Your cortisol is supposed to be high in the morning, to wake you up, and low at night, to let you sleep. But your curve is flat. Or it is inverted. Or it is just low all the time. The practitioner calls this "adrenal fatigue" or "HPA axis dysfunction." 

But what is it really? It is the exhaustion of a system that has been screaming for years. It is the result of waking up every morning and bracing yourself against a world that feels unsafe. It is the result of lying in bed at 2:00 AM, your mind racing with all the things you should have said, all the boundaries you should have drawn, while your body hums with a low-grade, unacknowledged rage. Your adrenals are not broken; they are on strike. 

**The Thyroid Panel:** Your TSH is high. Your free T3 is low. Maybe you have antibodies. Hashimoto's. The practitioner talks about iodine and selenium and gluten. 

But where is the thyroid located? It is in the throat. The throat is the center of expression. It is where the voice lives. What happens to the throat when you have something to say, but you swallow it instead? The muscles constrict. The energy gets blocked. The thyroid, sitting right there in the middle of the constriction, bears the brunt of the suppression. How many times have you felt that lump in your throat, that tightness, that physical sensation of holding back your truth? That is not just a metaphor. That is a somatic reality. 

**The Gut Permeability:** Leaky gut. Dysbiosis. The practitioner prescribes probiotics and bone broth. 

But the gut is the seat of the enteric nervous system. It is where we feel our deepest instincts. "Gut feeling." When you are constantly overriding your gut feelings—when your gut says "no" but your mouth says "yes"—you are creating a state of chronic conflict in your digestive tract. The blood flow is restricted. The mucosal lining is compromised. The gut becomes permeable because the boundaries of your life are permeable. You let too much in, and you cannot keep the toxic out. 

## The Herbs as Allies, Not Fixes

I am an herbalist. I believe in the power of plants. I believe in the medicine of the earth. But I do not believe that herbs are a substitute for doing the work of feeling your feelings. 

If you take [Gaia Herbs Adrenal Health Nightly Restore (120 caps)](https://www.amazon.com/dp/B01DA54H9G?tag=spankyspinola-20) to lower your cortisol, but you do not change the circumstances that are causing your cortisol to spike, you are just putting a band-aid on a bullet wound. The herb will help. It will give your nervous system a buffer. But it will not do the work for you. 

Herbs are allies. They are teachers. When you take a nervine like skullcap or a adaptogen like holy basil, you are inviting the energy of that plant into your body. You are asking it to support you. But you must also be willing to listen to what the plant is showing you. 

When the skullcap relaxes your nervous system, what do you feel underneath the tension? When the holy basil clears the brain fog, what do you see that you were trying not to look at? 

Often, when the chronic tension begins to release, the first thing that comes up is the anger. The rage that was held in the tight muscles, in the shallow breath, in the clenched jaw, suddenly has room to move. This can be terrifying. You might feel like you are losing your mind. You might feel like you are going to explode. 

This is where the real work begins. 

## The Practice of Somatic Tracking

Bessel van der Kolk, a psychiatrist and researcher who has revolutionized our understanding of trauma, emphasizes that the body keeps the score. In his seminal work, he explains that trauma and chronic stress are not just stored in the mind; they are encoded in the body's physiology. The nervous system becomes stuck in a state of defense, unable to return to baseline. 

Van der Kolk teaches that talk therapy alone is often insufficient for healing these deep somatic patterns. We cannot simply think our way out of a physiological state. We have to feel our way out. We have to learn to track the sensations in our bodies, to tolerate the discomfort, and to allow the trapped energy to move and discharge. 

This is the practice of somatic tracking. It is the practice of turning your attention inward and listening to the language of the body. 

### A Short Embodied Practice: Meeting the Tension

Find a quiet place where you will not be disturbed. Sit or lie down in a comfortable position. Close your eyes if that feels safe, or keep them open with a soft gaze. 

Take a few slow, deep breaths. Do not try to force the breath; just notice it. Notice where it goes in your body, and where it stops. 

Now, bring your attention to your jaw. Specifically, the masseter muscle, the thick muscle at the back of your jaw that you use for chewing. Notice if there is tension there. Do not try to relax it. Just notice it. 

Say to the tension, silently or out loud: "I feel you. I know you are there." 

Move your attention down to your neck and shoulders. The trapezius muscles, the levator scapulae. Notice the tightness. Notice how the shoulders might be creeping up toward the ears. Again, do not try to fix it. Just acknowledge it. "I feel you. I know you are there." 

Move your attention to your chest, your diaphragm, your belly. Notice any constriction, any holding, any numbness. 

Now, ask the tension a question. Ask it: "What are you protecting me from?" 

Wait for an answer. The answer might not come in words. It might come as an image, a memory, a sudden emotion, or a shift in sensation. Whatever comes, allow it. Breathe into it. 

If anger arises, let it arise. Notice where it lives in your body. Does it feel hot? Does it feel cold? Does it have a color? Does it have a shape? 

Stay with the sensation for as long as you can tolerate it. If it becomes too overwhelming, open your eyes, look around the room, and name three objects you can see. Ground yourself in the present moment. 

This practice is not about getting rid of the anger. It is about building the capacity to hold it, to feel it, and to let it move through you. 

## The Integration of Medicine and Movement

Functional medicine has its place. The labs are useful data points. The supplements can provide necessary support. If your vitamin D is low, take the vitamin D. If your B12 is depleted, take the B12. Use the [Solaray Total Calm Advanced](https://www.amazon.com/dp/B000Z1I5EC?tag=spankyspinola-20) to help your muscles relax at night. 

But do not mistake the protocol for the cure. 

The cure is the integration of the physical and the emotional. It is the willingness to look at the lab results and say, "Yes, my body is exhausted. And it has every right to be." 

It is the willingness to use the herbs and the supplements not to suppress the symptoms, but to support the body as it does the hard work of processing the unexpressed emotion. 

When you take your [Gaia Herbs SleepThru](https://www.amazon.com/dp/B005H1ENTQ?tag=spankyspinola-20) to calm your mind, use that calm not to dissociate, but to drop deeper into your body. Use the space created by the supplement to feel the anger that is sitting in your diaphragm. 

This is the true functional medicine. It is a medicine that functions on all levels—physical, emotional, and energetic. It is a medicine that honors the intelligence of the body, even when that intelligence manifests as disease. 

## The Fire in the Belly

In Traditional Chinese Medicine, anger is associated with the Liver meridian. The Liver is responsible for the smooth flow of Qi, or energy, throughout the body. When anger is repressed, the Liver Qi becomes stagnant. This stagnation can manifest as physical symptoms: headaches, menstrual irregularities, digestive issues, muscle tension. 

The Liver is also associated with the Wood element, with the energy of spring, of upward growth, of pushing through the soil. Anger, in its healthy form, is this Wood energy. It is the force that allows a seed to break open and push its way toward the sun. It is the force that allows you to set a boundary, to say "no," to demand what you need. 

When you suppress your anger, you are suppressing your own growth. You are keeping the seed buried in the dark. 

The workup in the functional medicine office is a map of where the seed has been buried. The low cortisol, the high antibodies, the permeable gut—these are the signs of a body that is trying to grow, but is being held back by the weight of unexpressed emotion. 

To heal, you must allow the fire in the belly to burn. You must allow the Liver Qi to move. You must allow the anger to rise up and break through the soil. 

This does not mean you have to yell at your boss or throw plates at the wall. It means you have to feel the anger in your body. You have to let the heat rise in your chest. You have to let the energy move through your limbs. You have to acknowledge the truth of your own experience. 

## The Return to Wholeness

Healing is not about returning to a state of pristine, unblemished health. It is not about achieving perfect lab results or a perfectly regulated nervous system. 

Healing is about returning to wholeness. It is about reclaiming all the parts of yourself that you have exiled, including the angry parts, the messy parts, the parts that refuse to comply. 

When you sit in the practitioner's office and they hand you the protocol, take it. Thank them for their expertise. But know that the real medicine is inside you. The real medicine is your willingness to feel your own fury, to honor your own boundaries, and to listen to the wisdom of your own body. 

Your anger is not a pathology. It is a messenger. It is telling you that something needs to change. It is telling you that you are worth protecting. 

Listen to it. Feel it. Let it move you. 

## FAQ

**Q: My functional medicine doctor says my anger is just a symptom of my hormone imbalance. Is that true?**

**A:** It is a two-way street. Yes, hormonal imbalances, particularly fluctuations in estrogen and progesterone, can lower your threshold for stress and make you more reactive. But the chronic suppression of anger and the resulting state of sympathetic dominance is often what causes the hormonal imbalance in the first place. Your body is a single, integrated system. You cannot separate the emotion from the physiology. Treat the hormones, yes, but also do the somatic work to process the anger. 

**Q: I feel like if I start letting myself feel my anger, I will never stop. It feels too big.**

**A:** This is a very common fear. When you have been holding a beach ball under water for twenty years, the idea of letting it go is terrifying. You fear it will explode and destroy everything. This is why somatic tracking is so important. You do not have to feel all the anger at once. You can titrate it. You can feel a little bit of the tension in your jaw, acknowledge it, and then step back. You build capacity slowly, over time. You are not opening the floodgates; you are just turning the valve a fraction of an inch. 

**Q: Can herbs really help with emotional processing?**

**A:** Absolutely. Herbs work on the physical tissues, but they also work on the energetic and emotional levels. A plant like Rose (Rosa spp.) can help soften the heart and ease the grief that often sits underneath anger. A plant like Blue Vervain (Verbena hastata) is specific for the person who holds their tension in their neck and shoulders, who is rigid and uncompromising, who needs to learn to let go of control. Herbs are not magic pills, but they are powerful allies in the work of somatic healing. 

**Q: What if my lab results are completely normal, but I still feel terrible?**

**A:** Standard lab ranges are based on a statistical average of a sick population. They are not a measure of optimal health. Furthermore, labs are just a snapshot in time. They do not capture the dynamic, lived experience of your body. If you feel terrible, your body is telling you something, regardless of what the paper says. Trust your somatic experience over the clinical data. Your body knows what it feels. 

**Q: How do I know if my physical symptoms are caused by repressed anger or something else?**

**A:** You don't always know for sure, and it is rarely just one thing. But if you have chronic tension in the jaw, neck, or shoulders; if you have digestive issues that flare up under stress; if you have a history of autoimmune conditions; and if you know, deep down, that you have a hard time setting boundaries or expressing displeasure, then repressed anger is likely a significant piece of the puzzle. Start with the somatic tracking practice. See what arises when you meet your physical symptoms with curiosity instead of an agenda to fix them.

## The Anatomy of Resentment

Let us look deeper into the fascia, the connective tissue that wraps every muscle, every organ, every nerve in your body. Fascia is not just inert wrapping paper. It is a highly innervated, dynamic communication network. It is rich in sensory receptors, particularly those that respond to mechanical stress and emotional tension. 

When you experience a boundary violation and you do not respond, the tension does not just sit in the muscle belly. It gets woven into the fascia. Over time, the fascia thickens, becomes rigid, and loses its glide. This is the physical manifestation of resentment. Resentment is anger that has been left to harden. It is the calcification of unexpressed fury. 

You feel it when you try to stretch and your body resists. You feel it in the chronic stiffness of your lower back, in the tight band across your chest that restricts your breathing. The functional medicine practitioner might call it systemic inflammation. They might prescribe curcumin or boswellia. And those herbs will help reduce the inflammatory markers. But they will not unweave the resentment from your fascia. 

To do that, you have to bring movement and breath into the hardened places. You have to meet the rigidity with compassion. You have to acknowledge the stories that are trapped in the tissue. The time you were passed over for the promotion. The time your boundaries were ignored. The time you were told you were being "too sensitive." 

These stories are not just in your head. They are in your fascia. And they require your attention. 

## The Role of the Psoas

Consider the psoas muscle, the deep core muscle that connects your lumbar spine to your femur. It is the only muscle that connects the upper body to the lower body. It is intimately involved in the fight-or-flight response. When you are startled or threatened, the psoas contracts, pulling you into a fetal position to protect your vital organs. 

In a state of chronic stress, the psoas never fully releases. It remains in a state of low-grade contraction, ready to spring into action. This chronic contraction pulls on the lower back, compresses the digestive organs, and restricts the movement of the diaphragm. 

When you are holding onto anger, your psoas is holding on with you. It is bracing for the impact that never comes. 

Releasing the psoas is not just a matter of physical stretching. It is an emotional release. When the psoas finally lets go, it is common for people to experience shaking, crying, or sudden waves of anger. This is the nervous system discharging the trapped energy. It is the body finally completing the stress cycle that was interrupted years ago. 

This is why somatic work is so profound. It bypasses the cognitive mind and goes straight to the tissue where the truth is held. 

## The Wisdom of the Night

Pay attention to what happens in your body at night. The functional medicine doctor will ask about your sleep hygiene. They will tell you to turn off your screens, to keep the room cool, to take your magnesium. 

But what happens when you wake up at 3:00 AM, your heart pounding, your mind racing? In Traditional Chinese Medicine, the hours between 1:00 AM and 3:00 AM are the time of the Liver. This is when the Liver meridian is most active, when the body is trying to process and clear toxins—both physical and emotional. 

If you are consistently waking up during this time, feeling agitated, hot, or anxious, it is a strong somatic signal that your Liver Qi is stagnant. The anger that you successfully suppressed during the day, when your conscious mind was in control, rises to the surface when your defenses are down. 

Do not just take a sleeping pill and try to force yourself back into unconsciousness. Lie there in the dark and listen. Where is the heat in your body? Where is the tension? What is the anger trying to tell you in the quiet of the night? 

The night is not your enemy. It is the time when the body speaks its most unvarnished truth. 

## The Courage to Be Unhealed

There is a tremendous pressure in the wellness world to be "healed." To have perfect labs, a perfectly regulated nervous system, a perfectly serene disposition. This pressure is just another form of the same cultural demand for compliance that made you sick in the first place. 

It is okay to be angry. It is okay to be dysregulated. It is okay if your labs are a mess. 

Your worth is not contingent on your health. Your value is not determined by your cortisol levels. 

The goal of this work is not to become a perfectly functioning machine. The goal is to become a fully alive human being. And human beings are messy. We get angry. We get hurt. We hold tension in your jaws and our psoas and our fascia. 

The courage is not in fixing it all. The courage is in feeling it all. It is in sitting in the practitioner's office, looking at the red and yellow bars on the lab report, and saying, "Yes. This is the map of my survival. And I am ready to read it."
